While four out of five software companies expect AI agents to noticeably change the software landscape, nearly a third of enterprises are not yet technically ready to embed the technology. Half are in the experimental phase, and a quarter are not yet doing anything with agentic AI. A large majority of software companies expect that the software landscape will noticeably change due to 'AI agents' - smart programs that can independently perform complex tasks. Nearly half of those surveyed even expect drastic or fundamental changes in the way software is designed, used or controlled, according to the survey by ABN AMRO and Panelwizard among 204 Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) companies.

text: Samir Selimi, founder of software company PurchAIse, has also put forward an AI agent on his platform. A different dashboard for each user. More than a quarter of SaaS companies see benefit in ad hoc generated screens. “Then you tell an agent: the window must be green, with corners and no roundings. And suddenly there is a screen, adapted to your preferences,” illustrates Franklin Evers of software company Usere light-heartedly. The company wants to keep its 'front-end', the part that the user sees, flexible in any case.

text: Microsoft lets companies build their own AI agents with Copilot Studio, which can call not only Microsoft apps but also external software. Salesforce developed a similar infrastructure with Agentforce. One in five software companies is considering connecting to such AI agent platforms. A quarter do not focus on specific platforms, but want to make their own software 'agent-ready' in a broader sense. These entrepreneurs make functions available in a modular manner, provide clear documentation, and design their software in such a way that instructions from external agents - whether or not in natural language - can easily be translated into concrete actions within the application. “As a software company, you have to ensure that LLMs can talk to your back end,” summarizes Dennis De Reus of Stellar.
